findings.title: BVI Economic Substance Filings Move to VIRRGIN Platform for 2026
findings.summary: The BVI International Tax Authority is transitioning economic substance filings from the BOSS system to the new VIRRGIN platform for reports due in and after 2026. From January 1, 2025, mandatory reporting distinctions apply between active and passive holdings. Reports must be filed within six months after financial year end through registered agents.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients owning BVI companies with a December 31, 2025 financial year-end must file their economic substance returns via the new VIRRGIN platform by June 30, 2026 — tomorrow — through their registered agent. Failure to file exposes the company to BVI ITA penalties and potential loss of good standing, and the platform transition from BOSS to VIRRGIN adds procedural risk for first-time VIRRGIN filers.
findings.urgency_reason: For calendar-year BVI companies, the six-month window from December 31, 2025 closes June 30, 2026 — tomorrow. Clients with BVI structures must confirm immediately with their registered agent that the filing has been or will be submitted today.

findings.title: Luxembourg Pillar Two: GIR Registration and Filing Deadline June 30, 2026
findings.summary: Luxembourg entities in scope of Pillar Two since January 1, 2024 with a calendar financial year must register with the Luxembourg tax authorities (ACD) by June 30, 2026 and file their GloBE Information Return (GIR) via xml upload on MyGuichet.lu. The ACD updated its Pillar Two FAQs on June 4, 2026, clarifying registration obligations and the filing exemption regime.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are UBOs of multinational groups with Luxembourg entities and €750M+ consolidated revenues must ensure their Luxembourg entities have registered with the ACD and are prepared to file their GloBE Information Return by June 30, 2026; the ACD's FAQ updates as recently as June 4 indicate ongoing compliance uncertainty that adds execution risk.
findings.urgency_reason: Filing deadline is June 30, 2026 — tomorrow. Luxembourg entities in scope that have not yet registered must act today. Late registration or non-filing carries penalties from the ACD.

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two: First GIR Filings Due June 30, 2026; GIR XML Schema Guidance Released
findings.summary: The first GloBE Information Return (GIR) filings for calendar-year multinational groups (€750M+ consolidated revenues) are due June 30, 2026, in Luxembourg and other key jurisdictions. The OECD released guidance on the GIR XML Schema for the first GloBE filing and exchange cycle in June 2026. The Netherlands opened Pillar Two filing on June 2, 2026.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are UBOs of multinational enterprise groups with €750M+ consolidated revenues face their first mandatory Pillar Two GIR filing cycle — due tomorrow in Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and other implementing jurisdictions. Many in-scope groups are navigating the XML submission process for the first time, and the late-breaking OECD schema guidance underscores the need for immediate engagement with tax counsel to avoid filing errors.
findings.urgency_reason: Filing deadline is June 30, 2026 — tomorrow. This is the first mandatory GIR cycle with no grace period. In-scope MNE groups that have not registered or prepared their GIR must escalate to their tax advisors immediately.

findings.title: CRS 2.0: Crypto-Assets, E-Money and CBDCs In Scope from January 1, 2026; Multiple June 30, 2026 Reporting Deadlines
findings.summary: OECD CRS 2.0 amendments took effect January 1, 2026 across participating jurisdictions. The Depository Account definition now encompasses e-money and CBDCs; financial institutions must report additional data elements. Multiple CRS reporting deadlines fall June 30, 2026 across jurisdictions including Guernsey, Isle of Man, Jersey, Luxembourg, and others. From January 1, 2027, CRS and FATCA reporting must be submitted separately.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with accounts across Guernsey, Isle of Man, Jersey, Luxembourg, and other CRS jurisdictions will have their 2025 account data — including newly in-scope crypto asset, e-money, and CBDC balances — automatically exchanged with their home tax authorities within weeks of the June 30 filing. Clients whose offshore crypto or e-money holdings have not been disclosed to their home tax authority face immediate exposure once exchange occurs.
findings.urgency_reason: Multiple CRS 2.0 filing deadlines across major wealth management jurisdictions fall June 30, 2026 — tomorrow. The first reporting year under expanded CRS 2.0 scope means tax authorities will receive significantly more data than in prior years. Any HNW client with undisclosed crypto or e-money balances in participating jurisdictions should seek urgent tax counsel today.

findings.title: JFSC Consults on Pillar 2 and Basel III; Jersey CRS Reporting Deadline June 30, 2026
findings.summary: The Jersey Financial Services Commission (JFSC) launched a consultation on Pillar 2 and prudential reporting requirements as part of its Basel III implementation programme. Jersey, Guernsey, and Isle of Man all face June 30, 2026 CRS reporting deadlines.
findings.client_impact: Jersey financial institutions — including family trust companies, fund structures, and any entity meeting the definition of a Reporting Financial Institution — must file their CRS 2025-data returns with the JFSC by June 30, 2026. HNW clients with Jersey trusts or holding structures should confirm their Jersey service provider has completed this filing.
findings.urgency_reason: CRS June 30, 2026 deadline is tomorrow. Late CRS filings carry JFSC penalties and may delay automatic exchange notifications to the relevant tax authorities. Clients with Jersey structures must confirm filing completion with their registered service provider today.

findings.title: Complementary Law 227/2026: Major ITCMD Inheritance Tax Reform
findings.summary: Enacted January 13, 2026, LC 227/2026 mandates progressive ITCMD rates across all states (max 8% in brackets based on market value at date of transfer), ends the constitutional gap on foreign assets held by Brazilian residents, and expressly subjects trust distributions and deemed transfers to ITCMD. PGBL private pension distributions are fully subject to ITCMD; VGBL only for contributions made within five years prior to death of the settlor.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian resident HNW clients with foreign trusts, PGBL pensions, or assets held abroad now face ITCMD exposure at progressive rates up to 8%; every trust distribution is now a potentially taxable event, and PGBL balances transferred at death are fully subject to ITCMD regardless of contribution date. Clients with VGBL policies should urgently audit contributions made within the past five years for potential estate tax exposure.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is already in force (January 13, 2026) with no 30-day deadline but the structural implications for trust distributions and foreign asset planning require advisory review at the next client meeting. The law has been in effect for over five months and clients with active trust distributions may already have unaddressed exposure.

findings.title: BCB Resolution 521/2025: Crypto/Stablecoin Flows Integrated into FX Regime
findings.summary: BCB Resolution No. 521/2025 (effective February 2, 2026) brings stablecoin flows and certain international crypto transfers under Brazil's foreign exchange compliance framework. VASPs must submit monthly reports on stablecoin purchases, sales, exchanges and counterparty identification for capital controls and AML purposes. Transaction limits apply: USD 100,000 per transaction for pure VASPs, USD 500,000 for FX-authorised financial institutions.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using stablecoins or crypto for international capital transfers from Brazil now face monthly VASP reporting requirements and per-transaction limits; large international capital movements via stablecoins will be visible to the BCB for AML and capital control purposes. Clients who have relied on stablecoin flows as an informal FX channel should immediately review the transaction size and frequency of their flows with Brazilian counsel.
findings.urgency_reason: Regulation is already in effect (February 2, 2026); no 30-day deadline but clients engaged in cross-border stablecoin flows should ensure their VASP is compliant and review whether any past transactions exceeded reporting thresholds or per-transaction limits.

findings.title: BCB VASP Authorization Window Open: February to October 2026
findings.summary: Under BCB Resolution No. 520 (effective February 2, 2026), VASPs already operating in Brazil must apply to the Central Bank for formal authorisation by October 29, 2026. Applicants must demonstrate robust internal controls, cybersecurity protocols, and AML/CFT compliance. Existing operators (domestic and foreign) face this deadline or must cease operations.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding crypto assets at Brazilian VASPs face risk of asset access disruption if their VASP fails to obtain BCB authorization by October 29, 2026; clients with material crypto positions at domestic Brazilian exchanges should verify whether their platform has applied for authorization and consider diversifying custody to BCB-authorized FX institutions.
findings.urgency_reason: Authorization deadline is October 29, 2026 — outside the 30-day URGENT window but four months away. Clients with significant crypto holdings at Brazilian VASPs should begin custodian due diligence now to avoid a rushed asset transfer if their platform fails to obtain authorization.

findings.title: FinCEN BOI: Domestic US Entities Fully Exempt; Foreign Registrants Still Required
findings.summary: Under the March 2025 Interim Final Rule, all entities created under US law (and their UBOs) are exempt from BOI reporting under the Corporate Transparency Act. Only foreign entities registered to do business in a US state or tribal jurisdiction must report; those registered before March 26, 2025 had to file by April 25, 2025. FinCEN is expected to finalize the rule in 2026 but has not yet done so.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with foreign holding companies or offshore entities registered to do business in US states remain subject to CTA BOI reporting; if they registered before March 26, 2025, their April 25 filing deadline has already passed and any non-compliance should be remediated immediately. The rule remains interim with finalization expected in 2026, creating ongoing uncertainty about future scope.
findings.urgency_reason: The April 25, 2025 deadline for pre-existing foreign registrants has passed; classified as WATCH because ongoing compliance obligations remain for newly registering foreign entities and because rule finalization in 2026 may alter scope. Clients with foreign entities registered in US states should confirm their BOI filing status.

findings.title: One Big Beautiful Bill Act: Federal Estate Tax Exemption Permanently Set at $15M
findings.summary: Signed July 4, 2025, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act permanently raises the federal unified estate and gift tax exemption to $15 million per individual ($30 million for married couples) effective January 1, 2026, indexed for inflation thereafter. No sunset provision applies. The top marginal rate remains 40%. Annual gift tax exclusion remains $19,000 per recipient in 2026.
findings.client_impact: US HNW clients and non-residents with US-sited assets can now plan around a permanent $15M individual exemption — eliminating the prior sunset risk and creating significant front-loaded gifting and trust-transfer opportunities. Clients whose estate plans were designed around the prior $10M–$12.92M exemption are leaving meaningful planning capacity unused and should schedule estate plan reviews.
findings.urgency_reason: The permanent exemption is already in effect with no 30-day deadline; classified as WATCH because this is a confirmed, permanent legislative change that should drive proactive estate plan reviews and annual gifting strategies during the next planning cycle.

findings.title: Florida SB 264 Foreign Buyer Restrictions Upheld; Near-Total Ban on Chinese Nationals Continues
findings.summary: Florida SB 264 (effective July 1, 2023) was upheld by courts in November 2025 and remains fully in force. Chinese nationals face a near-total ban on real estate purchases; nationals of six other 'countries of concern' (Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria) face restrictions on agricultural land and property within 10 miles of military/critical infrastructure. All buyers must sign affidavits; violations carry forfeiture risk and criminal penalties.
findings.client_impact: Chinese national clients are effectively barred from purchasing Florida real estate, and any advisory work involving Florida property for nationals of the seven listed countries must include mandatory affidavit review and geographic restriction analysis before closing. Violations carry criminal penalties and forfeiture risk, meaning pre-transaction nationality diligence is non-negotiable.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is upheld and in force with no new deadline; classified as WATCH because any new Florida real estate transaction involving nationals from affected countries requires this compliance check. The November 2025 court upholding removes any residual legal uncertainty.

findings.title: Tokenised Funds Regulatory Framework Effective March 24, 2026
findings.summary: The Mutual Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 and Private Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 came into force on March 24, 2026, establishing clear rules for tokenised mutual funds and private funds whose interests are represented by digital equity tokens or digital investment tokens. CIMA has authority to inspect underlying technology and token transactions. As of May 2026, CIMA uses a questionnaire-based interim approval regime pending final rules.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with interests in Cayman tokenised funds should know that CIMA now has statutory authority to inspect token transactions and the underlying blockchain technology; fund managers structuring digital equity or investment token offerings in Cayman must apply under the interim questionnaire regime before final rules are published.
findings.urgency_reason: Framework is newly in force (March 24, 2026) with no 30-day action deadline; classified as WATCH because clients or fund managers using Cayman tokenised fund structures should engage Cayman counsel to assess compliance under the interim regime before CIMA finalises the rules.

findings.title: Cayman Islands CRS 2.0 Package: Crypto-Assets, E-Money, CBDCs Now In Scope
findings.summary: The Cayman Islands will implement the OECD CRS 2.0 amendments, with enhanced data integrity requirements and expansion to expressly include crypto-assets, e-money, and central bank digital currencies within CRS scope. Additional reporting data elements are required, and a switch-off provision allows gross proceeds reporting under CRS to be waived where CARF reporting applies.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding crypto assets, e-money, or CBDC interests through Cayman financial institutions will have these positions reported under CRS beginning with 2026 data; Cayman financial institutions managing funds or accounts must expand their CRS due diligence and reporting to capture these newly in-scope asset classes. Note: Cayman CRS filing deadlines may also fall June 30, 2026 per the broader OECD CRS 2.0 URGENT finding — clients should confirm the deadline with their Cayman service provider.
findings.urgency_reason: CRS 2.0 is in effect from January 1, 2026; classified as WATCH at the Cayman-specific level pending CIMA confirmation of the June 30, 2026 filing deadline. The June 30 CRS filing urgency is addressed in the OECD CRS 2.0 URGENT finding above.

findings.title: BVI Beneficial Ownership Register Fully Operational April 1, 2026; Legitimate Interest Access Regime
findings.summary: From April 1, 2026, any person may submit a written request to the BVI Registrar to inspect or obtain a copy of entries in the BO Register upon demonstrating 'legitimate interest' and payment of a fee. Access is limited to individuals with a 25%+ ownership interest or equivalent control. Exemption applications (US$50 fee) are available from January 2, 2026. This is not a fully public register.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with BVI structures should be aware that counterparties, regulators, journalists, or litigants may now request access to their beneficial ownership information by demonstrating legitimate interest — a standard broader than a fully public register but narrower than unrestricted access. Clients holding sensitive assets in BVI vehicles should consider applying for the US$50 exemption if eligible, and should review whether their BVI structures warrant enhanced confidentiality measures.
findings.urgency_reason: Register access opened April 1, 2026 with no 30-day deadline; classified as WATCH because the legitimate interest access regime creates an ongoing privacy risk for BVI structures. Exemption applications are available and clients should evaluate whether to apply.

findings.title: Luxembourg Public Country-by-Country Reporting: First Year is 2025, Report Due End-2026
findings.summary: For taxpayers with a December 31, 2025 financial year-end, 2025 is the first fiscal year subject to public CbCR reporting, with the corresponding report due by end of 2026. Under DAC9 (transposed December 17, 2025), in-scope multinational groups (€750M+ consolidated revenue) will file a standardised Top-up Tax Information Return enabling automatic exchange among EU Member States.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are UBOs of large multinational groups (€750M+ consolidated revenues) with Luxembourg or EU nexus will have their entity-level tax data publicly disclosed for the first time for fiscal year 2025; advisors should prepare clients for the transparency implications of public CbCR, including stakeholder and press scrutiny of effective tax rate variances across jurisdictions.
findings.urgency_reason: Report is not due until end-2026 but data being disclosed pertains to FY2025 already completed; classified as WATCH because clients should engage their advisors now to understand what will be publicly available and prepare communications strategies ahead of publication.

findings.title: FINMA Consults on Revised AMLO-FINMA; Sanctions/Embargo Compliance and UBO Rules Tightened
findings.summary: On May 12, 2026, FINMA launched a consultation (closed June 9, 2026) on a partial revision of its Anti-Money Laundering Ordinance (AMLO-FINMA). Key changes include measures to prevent violations of the Swiss Embargo Act, reinforced requirements to understand client ownership and control structures, and tightened rules on payable-through and sub-accounts. The revised ordinance is expected to take effect January 1, 2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Swiss bank accounts or wealth management relationships should anticipate enhanced scrutiny of their ownership and control structures — including full corporate organograms — and tighter monitoring of sub-account and payable-through arrangements. Swiss institutions will begin implementing the new standards in H2 2026 in preparation for the January 1, 2027 effective date.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation closed June 9, 2026; final revised ordinance expected January 1, 2027. Classified as WATCH because Swiss financial intermediaries will begin enhanced due diligence outreach in the second half of 2026. Clients with complex ownership structures should proactively prepare updated documentation.

findings.topic: Company Formation & Beneficial Ownership / Succession & Wealth Planning
findings.title: MAS Revised Single Family Office Framework Effective June 15, 2026
findings.summary: MAS's revised SFO framework took effect June 15, 2026, providing a structure-agnostic class exemption from licensing for qualifying SFOs that notify MAS of their operations and maintain a MAS-licensed bank account. SFOs must file a simple annual return with AUM and bank details. Existing SFOs have a one-year transition period (until June 15, 2027) to comply.
findings.client_impact: HNW families with Singapore Single Family Offices must now notify MAS of their operations and maintain a MAS-licensed bank account to retain the licensing exemption; existing SFOs have until June 15, 2027 to comply, but new SFOs establishing from June 15, 2026 must comply from inception. The simplified structure-agnostic framework reduces setup friction and should be evaluated by clients considering Singapore as a family office hub.
findings.urgency_reason: New framework took effect June 15, 2026 with a one-year transition period; no 30-day deadline for existing SFOs. Classified as WATCH because clients establishing new Singapore SFOs or reviewing existing ones should engage with the new notification and annual return requirements promptly.

findings.title: MAS AML/CFT Enhancements Across Asset and Wealth Management Ecosystem
findings.summary: MAS has introduced a suite of AML/CFT enhancements applying to Capital Markets Services licensees, Variable Capital Companies, trust companies, and other capital markets intermediaries, aligning Singapore's regime with FATF standards. The enhancements reflect a shift toward principle-based supervision focusing on governance, judgment, and culture rather than procedural checklists.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Singapore-based family office structures, Variable Capital Companies, or trust arrangements should expect more intensive, judgment-led onboarding and periodic review processes from their Singapore service providers, including deeper scrutiny of source of wealth and beneficial ownership structures.
findings.urgency_reason: Enhancements are rolling out with no single compliance deadline; classified as WATCH because clients may face more intensive due diligence processes at Singapore institutions in 2026, and advisors should prepare clients for enhanced documentation requests.

findings.title: FTA Releases Updated Corporate Tax Guide on Family Foundations (CTGFF1), June 10, 2026
findings.summary: The UAE Federal Tax Authority published an updated CTGFF1 on June 10, 2026, clarifying that a juridical person can be jointly owned by more than one Family Foundation and still qualify for Article 17 transparency treatment; single family offices providing investment management services are unlikely to qualify for the 0% rate as they typically fall outside regulatory oversight frameworks.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using UAE Family Foundations should note that joint ownership by multiple foundations can qualify for Article 17 transparency treatment, enabling efficient co-investment structures; however, single family offices providing investment management services are unlikely to qualify for the 0% corporate tax rate under the updated guidance, and affected clients must restructure or face the standard 9% rate.
findings.urgency_reason: Updated CTGFF1 published June 10, 2026 — three weeks ago. Classified as WATCH because clients relying on Article 17 treatment or the 0% SFO rate should have UAE counsel urgently review their structure against the new clarifications, particularly regarding SFO investment management services.

findings.title: UAE Mandatory Audit for Tax Groups Effective for FY2025; E-Invoicing Pilot July 2026
findings.summary: From tax periods beginning January 1, 2025 (filings due 2026), all UAE tax groups must prepare Audited Special Purpose Financial Statements regardless of income level (the previous AED 50M threshold is eliminated). QFZP status companies in Free Zones must also be audited. Separately, mandatory e-invoicing (PINT-AE XML format) pilots in July 2026 for large businesses (AED 50M+ revenue); mandatory from January 1, 2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with UAE tax groups must now prepare and submit audited Special Purpose Financial Statements for FY2025 — regardless of income level — and QFZP free zone companies also face mandatory audit; those with AED 50M+ revenue businesses should begin e-invoicing readiness as the mandatory pilot starts next month and full mandatory implementation applies January 1, 2027.
findings.urgency_reason: FY2025 audit requirement is already applicable with corporate tax filings due in 2026; classified as WATCH with near-term urgency on the e-invoicing pilot (July 2026 — next month). Clients should confirm their UAE tax group audit status and engage e-invoicing readiness assessments without delay.

findings.title: Panama SBP Acuerdo 01-2026: Enhanced UBO Disclosure at 10% Threshold for Banks
findings.summary: Panama's Superintendency of Banks published Acuerdo No. 01-2026 in January 2026, raising AML/KYC standards for account opening. The Agreement requires identification and verification of all UBOs with 10%+ ownership or effective control (previously 25%), with certified passports, proof of address, full corporate organograms, AML policy manuals, and independent AML audit reports within 12 months. Abbreviated declarations are no longer accepted.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients banking in Panama through complex holding structures must now provide full KYC documentation for any person holding 10%+ ownership or effective control — a significantly lower threshold than the prior 25% standard — and existing account holders without updated documentation face account review requests from their Panamanian banks.
findings.urgency_reason: Acuerdo 01-2026 took effect January 2026; classified as WATCH because clients with Panama bank accounts should proactively verify their account documentation has been updated to the new standards before their bank initiates a periodic review request.

findings.topic: International Tax / Company Formation & Beneficial Ownership
findings.title: Cyprus Comprehensive Tax Reform Effective January 1, 2026
findings.summary: Cyprus parliament approved a sweeping tax reform on December 22, 2025, effective January 1, 2026. Key changes: (1) All Cyprus-registered companies are now automatically tax resident regardless of management and control location; (2) Deemed Dividend Distribution rules abolished for post-2025 profits; (3) SDC rate on actual dividends from post-2026 profits reduced from 17% to 5%; (4) All individuals aged 25+ must file annual tax returns; (5) Tax authorities gain expanded powers to request banking information covering six years.
findings.client_impact: The elimination of the management-and-control tax residency test for Cyprus companies fundamentally changes the planning landscape — clients who relied on Cyprus holding companies managed from abroad for non-Cyprus-resident treatment now have Cyprus-resident companies. Separately, the SDC rate reduction from 17% to 5% on actual dividends from 2026 profits creates a significant distribution planning opportunity, while expanded tax authority powers to access six years of banking information increases transparency risk for clients with Cyprus structures.
findings.urgency_reason: Reform is already in effect (January 1, 2026); classified as WATCH because the automatic tax residency change may have immediate and retroactive implications for existing Cyprus holding structures that were managed from outside Cyprus. Clients should have Cyprus counsel review their structures as a matter of priority.

findings.title: Bahamas Usufruct Interest Act 2026 Enacted March 23, 2026
findings.summary: The Bahamas enacted the Usufruct Interest Act, 2026 on March 23, 2026, establishing the first comprehensive statutory framework for usufructs in the jurisdiction. The Act enables separation of the right to use/enjoy/receive income from an asset from its legal ownership. Covers equity interests, securities, IP rights, digital assets, receivables, insurance policy rights, and real/personal property. Usufructs may last up to 99 years for natural persons and 30 years for legal entities. Digital assets are expressly included.
findings.client_impact: The Bahamas Usufruct Interest Act creates a powerful new succession planning tool for HNW clients: income or use rights over equity stakes, digital assets, and real property can now be carved off from legal ownership for up to 99 years. This is particularly valuable for clients with significant crypto holdings who want to transfer legal ownership to heirs while retaining income rights — a structure previously unavailable in most offshore jurisdictions.
findings.urgency_reason: Enacted March 23, 2026; classified as WATCH as a new planning opportunity rather than a compliance obligation. Advisors should actively consider usufruct structures for clients with digital asset succession planning needs and present the option at the next advisory meeting.

findings.title: FIRPTA: No Legislative Changes; Electronic Payment Mandate Forthcoming
findings.summary: FIRPTA withholding rates remain unchanged in 2026: 15% on gross sales price (10% if buyer uses as personal residence and price ≤$1M; exempt if price ≤$300K). The IRS is expected to mandate all FIRPTA withholding payments to be submitted electronically via EFTPS. Form 8288/8288-A must be filed within 20 days of sale.
findings.client_impact: Foreign clients selling US real property continue to face 15% FIRPTA withholding on gross sales price under the existing rate structure; the forthcoming EFTPS electronic payment mandate will affect procedural compliance but rates and filing timelines remain unchanged, so no structural action is needed.
findings.urgency_reason: No legislative changes; classified as FYI as background awareness for any foreign client selling US real property in 2026. The EFTPS mandate is forthcoming but not yet implemented, and no specific effective date has been announced.

findings.title: CIMA Fund Annual Return Fee Increased 28% from January 1, 2026
findings.summary: CIMA increased its Fund Annual Return (FAR) filing fee from CI$350 (~US$427) to CI$450 (~US$549) per fund annually, effective January 1, 2026, a ~28% increase. This applies to all registered mutual funds and private funds. Fund managers should update budget forecasts for 2026 compliance costs.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cayman-registered mutual or private funds will see a ~28% increase in annual CIMA filing fees, from approximately US$427 to US$549 per fund; this is a minor cost increase with no structural impact but should be reflected in 2026 fund operating budgets where not already incorporated.
findings.urgency_reason: Fee increase is already in effect and immaterial to investment thesis; classified as FYI as a routine cost-of-compliance item for fund managers to update in budget forecasts.

findings.title: FINMA Circular 2026/1: Climate and Nature-Related Financial Risk Now Binding
findings.summary: FINMA Circular 2026/1 (issued December 17, 2024) introduces binding expectations for climate and nature-related financial risk management. Category 1 and 2 institutions (largest banks and insurers) must comply by January 2026; Categories 3–5 must comply by January 2027. This elevates sustainability risk from best practice to a supervisory requirement for wealth management institutions.
findings.client_impact: Swiss custodian banks and asset managers serving HNW clients are now under binding FINMA supervisory requirements for climate and nature-related risk management, which may influence how Swiss institutions assess and manage client portfolio exposure to climate-sensitive assets and credit facilities.
findings.urgency_reason: This is a supervisory standard for Swiss financial institutions, not a direct client compliance obligation; classified as FYI as background context for HNW clients whose Swiss custodian banks or asset managers operate under FINMA supervision.

findings.title: Jersey Companies Law Major Amendment Effective June 1, 2026
findings.summary: The Companies (Jersey) Amendment Law 2026 came into force June 1, 2026 — the first major primary-law update since 2014. Key changes: par value companies no longer need to state maximum authorised share capital in their memorandum; private company member threshold rule abolished; merger documentation requirements simplified; summary winding-up streamlined with interim distributions permitted before final solvency registration.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Jersey holding companies or fund structures benefit from reduced corporate housekeeping obligations — no maximum authorized share capital requirement in the memorandum, no risk of automatic reclassification as a public company upon exceeding 30 members, and streamlined winding-up procedures that allow interim distributions before final registration. These are favorable simplifications with no new compliance obligations.
findings.urgency_reason: Favorable simplification effective June 1, 2026 with no action deadline and no new compliance obligations; classified as FYI as a background improvement for existing and prospective Jersey structures.
